coredata.dc:title: Non-oxidation reduction strategy for highly selective detection of ascorbic acid with dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric signals
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hongyu
Liu, Yalan
Li, Haitao
Zhang, Youyu
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: Ascorbic acid (AA), one of the most important vitamins, acts a critical role in various physiological reactions and is involved in many diseases. Thus, the development of highly selectivity methods for detecting AA level with the presence of different reducing substances is of great significance in complicated serum matrix. In this paper, a non-oxidation reduction regulation (NRR) strategy was designed to construct a novel dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-readout assay for highly selective detection of AA. The strategy involves three processes: Ag+ oxidated o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophenazine (OPDox), AA inhibited the generation of OPDox and the AA was oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA). The specific performance of DHAA NRR, due to the condensation reaction between dicarbonyl group of DHAA and diamine group of OPD, that is confirmed by ESI-MS and 1H NMR spectra analysis, can remarkably enhance the selectivity of AA detection. Additionally, the proposed method based on NRR strategy not only provides a sensing platform for probing AA but also shows promising outlook in biomedical studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-oxidation reduction regulation
Dual-ratio
Dual-readout
O-Phenylenediamine
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric assay for AA
Investigation into the response mechanism for AA
Dual-ratio fluorescent and colorimetric assays for AA
Selectivity and anti-interference of the assay
Detection of AA in serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric detection of AA based on NRR strategy.

The UV–vis spectrum (A) and the fluorescence spectrum (λex = 370 nm) (B) of (a) 200 μM Ag+ and 1 mM OPD; (b) 200 μM Ag+ and 50 μM AA; (c) 1 mM OPD and 50 μM AA; (d) 200 μM Ag+, 1 mM OPD and 50 μM AA in the 37 °C at 30 min under Tris-HAc (10 mM pH = 7.0) buffer solution. The fluorescence spectrum of DFQ (C) and OPDox (D).

The absorbance spectra (A) and normalized fluorescent spectra (λex = 370 nm) (C) of 200 μM Ag+, 1.5 mM OPD mixture in the presence of different concentrations of AA. (B), (D) the related linear relationship over the concentration range from 1 to 100 μM AA, respectively. I 345 represents the absorbance at 345 nm of DFQ. I 425 represents the absorbance at 425 nm of OPDox. F 434 represents the fluorescence intensity at 434 nm of DFQ. F 564 represents to the fluorescence intensity at 565 nm of OPDox.

The fluorescence intensities ratios of the analytical solution in the presence of other control metal ions, reductive species or amino acids only (A) and coexistence of AA (B). Error bars are obtained from three parallel samples. λex = 370 nm. The relevant substances are: (a) K+ (1 mM); (b) Na+ (1 mM); (c) Zn2+ (1 mM); (d) Mg2+ (1 mM); (e) Fe3+ (1 mM); (f) Co2+ (1 mM); (g) Ca2+ (1 mM); (h) Al3+ (1 mM); (i) DA (50 μM); (j) GSH (5 μM); (k) Cys (50 μM); (l) UA (50 μM); (m) Ala (50 μM); (n) Lys (50 μM); (o) His (50 μM); (p) glucose (50 μM); (q) Glu (50 μM); (r) Tyr (50 μM); (s) Cl− (20 mM), (t) Cl− (20 mM) + Hg2 2+ (10 mM), (u) AA (50 μM).
Determination results of AA in diluted human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Non-oxidation reduction strategy for highly selective detection of ascorbic acid with dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric signals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, 410081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yalan Liu and the first author made equal contributions to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ascorbic acid (AA), one of the most important vitamins, acts a critical role in various physiological reactions and is involved in many diseases. Thus, the development of highly selectivity methods for detecting AA level with the presence of different reducing substances is of great significance in complicated serum matrix. In this paper, a non-oxidation reduction regulation (NRR) strategy was designed to construct a novel dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-readout assay for highly selective detection of AA. The strategy involves three processes: Ag+ oxidated o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophenazine (OPDox), AA inhibited the generation of OPDox and the AA was oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA). The specific performance of DHAA NRR, due to the condensation reaction between dicarbonyl group of DHAA and diamine group of OPD, that is confirmed by ESI-MS and 1H NMR spectra analysis, can remarkably enhance the selectivity of AA detection. Additionally, the proposed method based on NRR strategy not only provides a sensing platform for probing AA but also shows promising outlook in biomedical studies.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), known as vitamin C, is one of the most necessary vitamins for maintaining the body’s normal activities in many processes such as enzymatic reactions, collagen synthesis, scurvy prevention, and immune system enhancement [1,2]. AA is also capable of antioxygenation and resists the oxidative damage of lipids, DNA, and proteins [3–7]. Many studies show that the AA level in human body is closely related to plenty of diseases like Parkinson’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease, cardio-vascular disease and certain types of cancer [8,9]. Therefore developing an analytical method of AA with simplicity, reliability and high selectivity is vital for analytical applications and clinical disease diagnosis.

So far, a series of methods have been reported for AA detection, including titration with an oxidizing agent [10], electrochemistry [11–13], chromatography [14] and chemiluminescence [15], colorimetric [16,17], enzymology [18], and capillary electrophoresis [19]. Although the above-developed techniques are routine approaches for the analysis of AA and give the different insights, some technique challenges and inevitable defects are still remained. For example, electrochemical method is one of the main ways for AA detection, however the electrochemical oxidation peak potentials for dopamine (DA) and uric acid (UA) are similar to that for AA, which will seriously interfere the analysis of AA [13]. Colorimetry is probably not to meet the detective demand of sensitivity. Chromatography involves expensive instrumentation, time-consuming pretreatment sample processes and professional operation in spite of its high sensitivity. In contrast, fluorescence methods with simple instruments, low cost, rapid analysis, easy operations and less sample consumption have sparked tremendous interest in recent years [20–22]. Many fluorescent nanomaterials have been employed for precise analysis of AA, including metal nanocluster [23,24], carbon material [25], semiconductor quantum dots [26] and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) [27]. However, most of the above nanosensors only utilized the reducibility of AA to transform the fluorescence of fluorescent nanoprobes. Hence, some reducing substances in human serum such as UA, DA and glutathione (GSH) would disturb the monitoring results of AA [26,28,29]. In addition, these fluorescence assays generally required complex and time-consuming preparation processes such as the nano-material synthesis and modification. Furthermore, it is unpractical to detect analyte concentration by using a fluorescent probe with non-internal single emission band, because the single emission intensity may be affected by instrumental efficiency and measurement condition [30]. In contrast, ratiometric fluorescence methods based on the ratio of the dual emission intensities can alleviate most of the ambiguities by self-calibration of two emission bands [31]. Thus, selective analysis of AA in the presence of different reducing substances in complicated serum matrix employing a ratiometric fluorescent sensor without the aid of nanomaterials, which is still quite rare, will be highly valuable but even more challenging.

Herein, we designed a novel non-oxidation reduction regulation (NRR) strategy based on dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric signal to detect AA for the first time. As shown in Scheme 1 , the Ag+ ion could oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophennazine (OPDox) with the maximum fluorescence emission peak at 564 nm and the strong absorption peak at 425 nm. Once AA was introduced, it can react with Ag+ to inhibit the generation of OPDox and the AA was oxidized to DHAA. The as-produced DHAA can combine with OPD to form 3-(dihydroxyethyl)furo[3,4-b]quinoxaline-1-one (DFQ) with the maximum fluorescence emission peak at 434 nm and the strong absorption peak at 345 nm, allowing ratiometric fluorescence (F 434/F 564) and ratiometric colorimetric (I 345/I 425) dual-readout signal to quantitatively analyze AA. Meanwhile, we also use ESI-MS, UV–vis, fluorescence and 1H NMR spectra analysis to conclude the AA-based reaction mechanism and sensing principle. In addition, the visual and simple biosensor for AA detection based on NRR strategy can effectively avoid the interferences from reductive biological molecules. Moreover, the highly selective, cost-effective dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-readout assay has also been successfully applied to the detection of AA in complicated real samples.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained by UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimazu Co., Japan). A F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co.,Japan) was applied for the fluorescence analysis. Nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra of the compounds was measured by use of a Bruker AVB-500 spectrometer (in CDCl3, TMS as an internal standard). Electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) spectra was acquired on ZQ2000 mass spectrometer (Manchester, UK).

Ascorbic acid (AA), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), silver nitrate, acetic acid, sodium acetate, tris-methylamin, acetonitrile, potassium nitrate, sodium nitrate, zinc nitrate, calcium nitrate, cobalt nitrate, magnesium sulfate, iron sulfate, aluminum sulfate, mercury nitrate dihydrate, methyl alcohol were collected from Shanghai Reagent (Shanghai, China). Dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), alanine (Ala), lysine (Lys), histidine (His), glucose, glutamic (Glu), tyrosine (Tyr) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The buffer solutions with different pH were prepared by 0.01 M Tris-HAc. All other chemicals (99%, Merck) used in this work were of analytical grade and without further purification. Millipore Milli-Q ultrapure water (Millipore, ≥18 MΩ cm, USA) was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric assay for AA
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the sensitivity and selectivity tended for AA in the Ag+/OPD sensing system, different concentrations of AA, 200 μM AgNO3 and 1.5 mM OPD were mixed in the 37 °C for 30 min under the Tris-HAc (10 mM, pH = 7) buffer. Then the fluorescence and colorimetric spectra of the above incubated solution were obtained at room temperature (25 ± 1.0 °C). The selectivity of this sensing system for AA was investigated by using other biomolecules or common ions instead of AA.

According to a literature method [32], treating human serum samples (provided by the Hospital of Hunan Normal University) by acetonitrile to remove impurities is necessary before using in the experiment and then the product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove protein. The supernatant was diluted 10-fold and analysed with the developed method and then an exact concentration of AA (10, 30 and 50 μM) was respectively spiked to the serum samples to check the percentage recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation into the response mechanism for AA
ce:section.ce:para: The response mechanism involves three processes: Ag+ oxidated OPD to OPDox, AA inhibited the generation of OPDox and the AA was oxidized to DHAA, the as-produced DHAA can combine with OPD to form DFQ. The novel analytical method based on NRR strategy makes the sensing system superior to the routine method in selectivity. To demonstrate the NRR strategy can be realized, the reaction of DHAA with OPD was carried out in CDCl3. The product was separated by column chromatography and characterized by ESI-MS spectra and 1H NMR analyses. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the peak integrals (high field/down field = 4/4) are consistent with the total number and displacement of the hydrogen atoms in DFQ, except for the active hydroxyl. Additionally, using a mass spectrometric approach, characteristic ions can be produced by attaching anions to analyte molecules in the negative ion mode of ESI-MS. The ESI-MS spectrum showed a peak at 245.15 ([M−H]−) that was assignable to DFQ (246.06) shown in Fig. 1 . All above results indicated that AA can inhibit the production of OPDox and be oxidized to DHAA. The as-generated DHAA can combine with OPD to form DFQ in the AA/Ag+/OPD system. Hence, we confirm that the NRR strategy can be achieved.

To further evaluate the feasibility of this new sensing platform towards AA, we investigated the effects of the related factors. As presented in Fig. 2 , when there is only Ag+ and OPD in the solution, there is an characteristic absorption peak at 425 nm (Fig. 2A (a)) and a fluorescence emission peak at 564 nm (Fig. 2B (a)). The Ag+/AA, OPD/AA systems had no absorption peak at 345 nm (Fig. 2A (b, c)) and fluorescence emission peak at 434 nm (Fig. 2B (b, c)). However, upon addition of AA into the solution containing Ag+ and OPD, there were two strong absorption peaks at 345 nm and 425 nm as shown in the Fig. 2A (d), which attribute to DFQ and OPDox, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra were also studied (Fig. 2B (d)). And it exhibits two fluorescence emission peaks at 434 nm and 564 nm under the excitation wavelength at 370 nm (λex = 370 nm), which attribute to DFQ and OPDox, respectively. In addition, the fluorescence peaks of DFQ and OPDox excited by various irradiation nearly locate at 434 nm (Fig. 2C) and 564 nm (Fig. 2D), respectively, indicating excitation wavelength-independent properties. Consequently, all of above results indicated that the sensing system with dual-ratio fluorescence and colorimetric signals can be successfully applied to analysis of AA based on NRR strategy.

We optimize the pH, amount of OPD, temperature and incubation time in the sensing system for the purpose of improving the detection sensitivity. pH is one of the most necessary factors in our work above all, because it may influence the oxidation of AA and OPD. According to the previous reports, AA was oxidized more easily in the alkaline solution rather than the acidic solution [33]. Therefore, so as to realize the highly sensitive detection, we explored changes of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 434/F 564)of this system in the pH range of 7.0–9.0. Fig. S2A suggested that the F 434/F 564 reach the maximum at the pH 7.0, so we chose 7.0 as the optimal pH of our test. Besides, we further investigated the influence from OPD concentration for the sensing system. As shown in Fig. S2B, F 434/F 564 reached the maximum value when the OPD concentration was 1.5 mM, so we selected the optimal dosage as 1.5 mM in the detection system. In addition, we also investigated the effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 434/F 564). As shown in Fig. S2C, during the temperature increasing, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 434/F 564) increases first, then slowly decreases, and finally remains stable. Therefore, in order to obtain a stable signal, 37 °C was selected as the optimal reaction temperature. Finally, the incubation time was also optimized. As presented in Fig. S2D, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 434/F 564) was reduced with the increasing of incubation time (5–30 min) and reached a stable value at 30 min, which may be because the DFQ generation rate is less than the OPDox generation rate within 5–30 min. After 30 min of reaction, the amount of DFQ and OPDox produced remained unchanged, so the fluorescence intensity ratio remained stable. Thus we choose 30 min as the incubation time in order to obtain stable signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-ratio fluorescent and colorimetric assays for AA
ce:section.ce:para: The ultraviolet intensity ratio and emission intensity ratio change were recorded after adding different concentrations of AA from 1 to 100 μM by UV–vis spectra (Fig. 3 A) and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3C). As shown in Fig. 3A, the absorbance of OPDox at 425 nm decreased gradually and a new absorbance peak at 345 nm of DFQ appeared with the increasing concentration of AA, and the absorbance ratio (I 345/I 425) was found to be linear with the AA concentration in the range of 1–100 μM. The calibration curves can be expressed as I 345/I 425 = 0.01881 x − 0.05109 (1–60 μM, R2 = 0.977) (x represent the concentration of AA) and I 345/I 425 = 0.04579 x − 1.17024 (60–100 μM, R2 = 0.971), respectively (Fig. 3B). Noticeable color changes continuously from yellow to nearly colorless can be observed by naked eye (inset of Fig. 3B). The limit of detection (LOD) for AA was 0.69 μM (S/N = 3). Meanwhile, the fluorescent intensity of OPDox at 564 nm decreased and a new emission peak at 434 nm appeared with the increase of the AA concentration (Fig. 3C). A good linear relationship between F 434/F 564 and the concentration of AA was obtained in the range from 1 to 100 μM. As shown in Fig. 3D, the calibration curves for AA has good linearity for concentrations in the 1–50 μM and 50–100 μM range with the following equation: F 434/F 564 = 0.03494 x − 0.06021 (x represent the concentration of AA), R2 = 0.975 and F 434/F 564 = 0.09048 x − 2.93922, R2 = 0.993, respectively. The LOD is as low as 0.17 μM (S/N = 3). To our knowledge, this dual-ratio dual-readout assay showed lower LOD than many reported approaches (Table S1), which suggested that the developed sensing platform exhibits good performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of the assay
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is a key factor to evaluate the sensing property of the developed method, especially for the assay with potential applications in practical biological samples, because the physiological environment is complex, some reactive species such as ions, amino acids, other antioxidants and biomolecule would influence the analysis of AA. In order to investigate the selectivity of the system for AA, various potential interfering substances including K+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cl−, DA, GSH, Cys, UA, Ala, Lys, His, glucose, Glu and Tyr, respectively, were measured under the same conditions. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 A, the experimental results clearly showed that the influence of ions, proteins and reductive species on fluorescence intensity ratio (F 434/F 564) were negligible. However, there was dramatic increase of F 434/F 564 after the addition of AA, confirming the highly selective ability to AA. The above results showed that these related substances did not show interference in the detection of AA, this is mainly due to the strong reduction performance of AA and NRR performance of DHAA. In addition, changes of corresponding absorbance caused by these potential substances are similar to fluorescence and the data is not shown here. It is noteworthy to mention that GSH could restrain generation of OPDox, which is mainly due to the reduction activity of GSH and affinity for Ag+ [34]. However, considering that serum glutathione levels are only 3–5 μM [35], we made a GSH with low concentration (5 μM) interferometric test and found that GSH has a tiny impact on the experiment. In addition, Fe3+ can oxidize OPD under certain conditions [36], but the solubility product constant (Ksp) of Fe(OH)3 is 2.8 × 10−39, so that at pH 7.0, the concentration of Fe3+ of 2.8 × 10−18, which is not enough to interfere with our experiments. In order to further investigate the anti-interference ability and reliability of this assay, the OPD/Ag+ sensing system in the presence of AA along with other potential interference substances were determined. As expected, the coexistence of other relevant substances had negligible influence when recognizing AA in our expremental condition (Fig. 4B). However, it is noticeable that high concentrations of Cl− can produce insoluble salts with Ag+, which can interfere with the detection of target. But due to Hg2 2+ can form a more insoluble precipitated salt with Cl− (Hg2Cl2, Ksp = 1.3 × 10−18), interference problem of Cl− can be easily solved. All above results showed the detection principle of this approach was different from the traditional redox-based sensing strategies. In particular, the problem of reductive substance interference can be well solved by using the specificity of DHAA molecular NRR. This method can utilize not only the reductive characteristics of AA but also its oxidation product (DHAA) structural features, coupled with appropriate substrate (OPD), which showed a high specificity and high sensitivity for AA detection, and may be directly applied to detect AA in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to demonstrate the applicability of the assay to quantify AA in biological fluids, human serum samples were analyzed and shown in Table 1 . Taking into account the AA level in healthy human serum and the linear range of our method, the serum samples were diluted 10-fold before tested. After that, 10 μM, 30 μM, and 50 μM AA were spiked into the diluted serum samples, respectively, and then were tested. The experimental results exhibited that recoveries of AA reached to 92.99–107.48% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.41–3.49%, suggesting the applicability of the developed method to serum samples.

In conclusion, we have successfully developed a dual-ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-readout method for highly selective AA detection based on NRR performance of DHAA and the excellent optical property of DFQ and OPDox. The sensing assay with fluorescent and colorimetric dual modes could output a variety of signals and make the analytical results more convincing. In addition, this meticulous investigation combines with UV–vis, fluorescence, ESI-MS and 1H NMR spectra analysis on the reaction mechanism of AA regulation Ag+-triggered reaction of OPD for the first time. More importantly, the sensor also can be used for the detection of AA in real serum samples with satisfactory results and good repeatability.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874042 and 21475043).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.020.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hongyu Chen is a doctoral student in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, China.

Yalan Liu is a graduate student in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, China.

Haitao Li is a Professor in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University. He received his MSc degree from Hunan Normal University and Doctor degree from College of Chemistry, Nankai University, China. His research is focused on organic synthesis and chemical and biological sensing.

Youyu Zhang is a Professor in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University. She received her MSc degree from Hunan Normal University and Doctor degree from State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biological Sensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, China. Her research is focused on chemical and biological sensing. Her current research interests are in the areas nanomaterials and nanoanalysis.

Shuozhuo Yao is a Professor in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University and he is an academician of Chinese Academy of Science as well.
